coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of molecularly imprinted black TiO2-x/carbon dots nanocomposite and its recognizable photocatalytic performance under visible-light
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Jia
Zhang, Chuanxiang
Yang, Yulin
Su, Ting
Yi, Guiyun
Zhang, Xiuxiu
coredata.dc:description: In this work, novel molecularly imprinted black TiO2-x/CQDs (MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs) nanocomposites are firstly synthesized using methylene blue (MB) as molecular imprinter and reducer via hydrothermal-calcination method. MB has a double effect on the formation of MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs: Firstly, alter the morphology and structure, increasing the specific surface area and endowing the selective adsorption ability; Secondly, promote the formation of Ti3+ ions and oxygen vacancies, efficiently enhancing the visible light utilization and suppressing the photoelectrons-holes recombination process. The introduction of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is icing on the cake, further extends the optical response range, hinders the electrons-holes recombination process and heightens the photocatalytic performance. Multiple characterizations have been implied to analysis the structure, composition, chemical state, optical absorption property, photochemical characteristic and photocatalystic performance of as-prepared MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs nanocomposites. As a result, the remarkable performance of MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs for MB degradation under visible light is attributed to the broadened light-response range due to the Ti3+ ions and oxygen vacancies, increased specific surface area and selective adsorption achieved by molecular imprinting, efficient photogenerated carriers’ movement thanks to the CQDs modification and synergism among Ti3+ ions, oxygen vacancies and TiOH bonds.

Photocatalysis

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of molecularly imprinted black TiO2-x/carbon dots nanocomposite and its recognizable photocatalytic performance under visible-light

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, novel molecularly imprinted black TiO2-x/CQDs (MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs) nanocomposites are firstly synthesized using methylene blue (MB) as molecular imprinter and reducer via hydrothermal-calcination method. MB has a double effect on the formation of MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs: Firstly, alter the morphology and structure, increasing the specific surface area and endowing the selective adsorption ability; Secondly, promote the formation of Ti3+ ions and oxygen vacancies, efficiently enhancing the visible light utilization and suppressing the photoelectrons-holes recombination process. The introduction of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is icing on the cake, further extends the optical response range, hinders the electrons-holes recombination process and heightens the photocatalytic performance. Multiple characterizations have been implied to analysis the structure, composition, chemical state, optical absorption property, photochemical characteristic and photocatalystic performance of as-prepared MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs nanocomposites. As a result, the remarkable performance of MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs for MB degradation under visible light is attributed to the broadened light-response range due to the Ti3+ ions and oxygen vacancies, increased specific surface area and selective adsorption achieved by molecular imprinting, efficient photogenerated carriers’ movement thanks to the CQDs modification and synergism among Ti3+ ions, oxygen vacancies and TiOH bonds.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid development of modern industry not only brings us great convenience, but also deeply affects the ecological environment of our life. In recent years, high concentration of organic pollutants, drugs and personal care products as well as some highly toxic, refractory pollutants are discharged in rivers, seriously threatening the environmental ecological security and human health. In the chemical treatments of sewage, photocatalytic degradation has been widely paid attention to because of its mild reaction conditions and small secondary pollution [1–3].

Semiconductor photocatalysis is a promising waste-water purification technology. TiO2 as a commonly used photocatalyst has the characteristics of high catalytic activity, low cost, good chemical stability and so on [4,5]. But there are some defects, for instance, low utilization of sunlight, rapid recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes, and no selective degradation ability to pollutant molecules. Therefore, in the past decades, researchers have devoted much time and wisdom to tackle these problems, by means of noble metal deposition [6,7], surface photosensitization [8,9], metal/non-metal ions doping [10,11], construction of hetero-composite structures [12,13], etc.

In 2010, black TiO2 was firstly fabricated by hydrogenating TiO2 nanocrystals in H2 atmosphere, which provided superior optical property [14]. Thereafter preparing black TiO2 has been proved to be a potential method to enhance the light absorption and photocatalytic properties of TiO2. Furthermore, oxygen vacancies and Ti3+ can expand TiO2 absorption range and facilitate the separation of photogenerated carriers, thus further promoting the photocatalytic activities [15–17]. There are various ways to synthesize black TiO2, including high H2-pressure process, hydrogen thermal treatment, H2 or metal reduction and other methods [18–20]. However, these routes always require harsh reaction conditions or long reaction time. In addition, black TiO2 prepared by hydrogenation possesses limited porous structure and specific surface area, which are not conducive to photocatalytic performance. Therefore, convenient approaches are needed to prepare black TiO2 with high specific surface area.

Construction heterostructured composite is considered to be an effective method to enhance the photocatalytic performance of semiconductors. So far, varied heterojuctions have been constructed, for instance, g-C3N4/TiO2−x [21], carbon dots/C3N4 [22], carbon dots/TiO2 [23], carbon dots/Bi2WO6 [24], and showed remarkable advantages. Carbon dots (CQDs) as an emerging fluorescent carbon-based material has excellent optical properties, low toxicity, low cost, high chemical stability, efficient electron transfer property, etc. In most reports, CQDs/semiconductor hybrid photocatalysts exhibit excellent optical properties and photogenerated carriers transfer characteristics [25].

Selectivity of photocatalysts is also an important aspect of photocatalytic technology, especially when the target degradation pollutant in the presence of a complicated aqueous environment [26,27]. Although the photocatalytic degradation ability of modified TiO2 photocatalysts has been significantly improved, its photocatalytic oxidation is not selective because of the strong non-selective oxidizability of holes and hydroxyl radicals [26–29]. When TiO2 is used to treat polluted waste-water, high concentration of low toxic and/or non-toxic organic pollutants are firstly degraded, while low concentration of high toxic organic pollutants can not be effectively degraded [28,30,31]. Therefore, it is necessary to enhance the photocatalytic degradation ability and selectivity of TiO2 under visible light. Researchers have found several solutions to improve the photocatalytic selectivity of TiO2, which can be divided into four categories: adjusting pH to control the surface electric charge state; surface-grafting specific molecules to selectively adsorb target pollutants; designing double-zone structure photocatalytic complex with adsorption and photocatalytic activity regions; molecular imprinting technology [30]. Coating polymer organic layer with molecular imprinting on the TiO2 surface to have a specific affinity for target organic compounds is the most effective method to improve the photocatalytic selectivity. However, this method has some disadvantages: Firstly, the preparation steps are troublesome and require a large amount of solvent to remove imprinted molecules; Secondly, the organic polymer layer hinders light absorption and is unstable in the photocatalytic process; more importantly, the catalytic effect under visible light is not satisfied [32–34]. Recently, removing imprinted molecules by high temperature decomposition has been proposed to avoid these shortcomings except the drawback of inefficient photocatalytic activity under visible light [31,35–37].

Inspired by the above outstanding works, molecularly imprinted black TiO2-x/CQDs (MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs) with selectivity and high specific surface area were prepared via a facile two-step hydrothermal calcination method. The unique optoelectronic properties of carbon dots can be more effectively utilized by introducing it into black MIP-TiO2-x. On this basis, the further introduction of CQDs not only improved the optical absorption ability, but also promoted the formation of Ti3+ ions and oxygen vacancies in MIP-TiO2-x nanocomposites and effectively enhanced the separation of photogenerated carriers. As a result, the photocatalytic activity of MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs to methylene blue under visible light was significantly improved.
All chemicals were used without further purification and purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai).

Synthesis of CQDs: CQDs were synthesized based on our previous report [38] with some modifications. 1 g lignite was dispersed in 60 ml mixed solvent (H2O2 and formic acid with 1:5 vol ratio), stirred at 80 °C for 3 h. Filter the as-prepared product through 0.45 μm diameter pore. After that, 0.5 ml diethylenetriamine was added into the obtained liquid (about 45 ml), kept stirring for 15 min, transferred into 100 ml Teflon-lined autoclave and kept at 180 °C for 6 h. Then, the final product was dialyzed (MWCO 500 Da) for 48 h and freeze-dried to get the CQDs powder.

Synthesis of MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs: Adding 0.5 ml HNO3 into 60 ml deionized water and 0.03 g CQDs to form solution A, then 0.5 g methylene blue was added into the solution A. After stirring well, 6 ml Titanium (IV) isopropoxide was slowly dropped in, heat-stirred to 95–100 °C, maintained about 2 h until the solution become into 20 ml blue sol. Transfer the obtained blue sol to a 100 ml Teflon autoclave, heat to 200 °C and keep it there for 15 h. After cooling down, wash and centrifugal separate the final product repeatedly by deionized water and ethanol, dry and sinter it at 500 °C for 2 h, under nitrogen protection.

Synthesis of MIP-TiO2-x and TiO2: MIP-TiO2-x and the reference sample TiO2 were prepared through the above-mentioned procedure. But MIP-TiO2-x was synthesized without adding CQDs, and TiO2 was fabricated without adding methylene blue and CQDs.

Crystal structure was analyzed by X-ray diffractmeter (Bruker AXS D8 ADVANCE). BET surface area was measured via AutosorbeiQeMP instrument with N2. Morphology and microstructure were examined via scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Hitachi SU8020) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, EFI Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN). X-ray photoelectron spectra were recorded by Thermo Scientific Escalab 250Xi. The Renishaw inVia confocal Raman microscope was used to take Raman spectra. The PerkinElmer 750 spectrophotometer was applied to record the UV–Vis absorption spectra. Isoelectric point was measured by a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern Instruments). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained on Edinburgh FLS1000 fluorescence spectrometer with 350 nm excitation wavelength. The transient photocurrent density and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were taken by electrochemical work station (CHI760E). Electron spin resonance (ESR) measurements were carried out using Bruker A300-10/12 spectrometer, 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) was used as spin trap for hydroxide radical (•OH) and superoxide radical (
O2 −); electrons and holes were detected by 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine-1-oxyl (TEMPO) and 1-hydroxy-3-carboxy-2,2,5,5-tetramethylpyrrolidine (CPH).

0.1 g catalyst was dispersed in 200 ml of 10 mg/l MB aqueous solution and stirred in the dark for 1 h. A 300 W xenon lamp (PLS-SXE300DUV) with a 420 nm cut-off filter was used as visible light source, the test temperature was maintained at 25 °C. Every 20 min, samples were examined (filter through 0.22 μm microporous membrane and analyze the maximum absorbance of MB).

To evaluate the recognition selectivity of MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs towards MB, methyl orange (MO) was used as competitive substance. 0.1 g MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs was dispersed in 300 ml mixed solution (contain 10 mg/l MB and 10 mg/l MO). Both adsorption and photocatalytic tests were carried out, and samples were analyzed every 20 min (filter through 0.22 μm microporous membrane and analyze the maximum absorbance of MB and MO). The test conditions were consistent with the above photocatalytic activity test. The pH value of the experiments was not adjusted, and the initial value was about 6.8.

The following equation was used to calculate the adsorption capacity (Q):
Q =
(
C 0
-
C t
) × V
W
where C0 represents the initial concentration, Ct stands for the concentration at different time interval, V means the solution volume, W is the photocatalyst mass.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phase structure, morphology and BET analysis of catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns of TiO2, MIP-TiO2-x and MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs, respectively. These dominant diffraction peaks correspond to the standard phase of anatase TiO2 (JCPDS No. 21-1272), where the peaks at 25.3°, 36.9°, 37.8°, 38.6°, 48.0°, 53.9°, 55.1°, 62.7°, 68.7°, 70.3° and 75.0° are corresponding to (101), (103), (004), (112), (200), (105), (211), (204), (116), (220) and (215), respectively. And These peaks did not show obvious shift which suggested that the introduction of template molecules and carbon dots did not influence the crystal structure of TiO2. The average crystal sizes were 8.93 nm, 7.02 nm and 6.97 nm for TiO2, MIP-TiO2-x and MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs, respectively, calculating by the Scherrer’s formula on the anatase (101) diffraction peak. The results indicated the crystal size slightly decreased after introducing the template molecules and carbon dots. It is probably due to the fact that the template molecules and carbon dots are on the surface of TiO2, which constrained the crystal growth; or the disorder-elicited lattice strains [39].

SEM images are shown in Fig. 2 A–C, the morphology of TiO2, MIP-TiO2-x and MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs are almost spherical nanoparticles. From the HR-TEM images in Fig. 2D–2F, we can observe the lattice spacing of 0.35 nm in these three samples, corresponding to the (101) plane of anatase TiO2, which well agrees with the above XRD result. It is worth to notice that specific disordered surface layers which are marked with red arrows in Fig. 2E and F. The disordered layers observed in MIP-TiO2-x and MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs indicate the presence of surface defects [14,39–41]. The surface defects could be positive to enhance the photocatalytic activity of catalyst. As we know, Ti3+ and/or oxygen vacancies can improve the visible light response and act as electron capture centers to prevent the recombination of photogenerated carries, thereby promoting the photoelectrons utilization and enhancing the photocatalytic performance [14,39–41]. In Fig. 2F, carbon dots can be found on the surface of MIP-TiO2-x. To further confirm the introduction of carbon dots, we also studied the Raman spectrum of MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs (see Fig. S1). As we can see, four typical peaks at 151, 396, 514 and 638 cm−1, corresponding to the Eg(1), B1g(1), A1g + B1g(2) and Eg(3) modes of anatase TiO2 [42,43]. Except the TiO2 characteristic peaks, two additional peaks around 1339 and 1580 cm−1 can be seen, which correspond to the D and G band of carbon.

Specific surface area is a key property of photocatalyst, which correlates to the adsorption capability and photocatalytic activity. The aim of introducing imprinting molecules and then removing them is to produce imprinted cavities to the TiO2 surface, therefore increase the specific surface area. In Fig. 3 A, all these samples exhibited the typical type IV isotherms with H3 hysteretic loops at high relative pressure range between 0.4 and 1, which indicates the well-developed mesoporous structures [44,45]. This is also confirmed by its corresponding pore-size distribution (in Fig. 3B) calculated by BJH equation. These three pore distribution plots appear to be multi-model shape, which are caused by diverse aggregates and complicated pore structures existed in the composites [46,47]. The small mesopores arise from the intra-agglomeration of primary TiO2 nanocrystallites, while the large mesopores are relevant to larger inter-aggregated pores caused by the intra-aggregated secondary particles. From Fig. 3B, we can see the pore size distribution of TiO2 is wider than those of MIP-TiO2-x and MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs, which is in accordance with the XRD results, as calculated the crystal size of TiO2 is larger than these two imprinted composites. Compared with TiO2, it is obvious to see a pore size peak centered around 1.5 nm, which means the existence of micropores (<2 nm) in MIP-TiO2-x and MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs. This is ascribe to the added imprinting MB molecules, and further proves the formation of imprinted cavities on the TiO2 surface after removing the imprinted molecules. Due to the smaller crystal size and imprinted cavities, the MIP-TiO2-x has larger specific surface area (123.63 m2/g) than that of TiO2 (98.23 m2/g); and because of the introduction of carbon dots, MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs possesses a little bigger surface area (134.41 m2/g).

To verify the introduction of CQDs and formation of surface defects (Ti3+ and/or oxygen vacancies) after MB molecule imprinting, XPS and ESR were further conducted. In Fig. 4 A, the C1s spectra of TiO2, MIP-TiO2-x can be fitted into three peaks at 284.6, 285.7 and 288.5 eV, which are assigned to the CC/CC bond, CO bond and CO bond, respectively. After the introduction of carbon dots, an additional peak around 283.3 eV appeared in MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs, which is related to the formation of TiCO bond [48–50]. The appearance of TiCO bond demonstrated the existence of CQDs and its unique chemical connection with MIP-TiO2-x.

The binding energies of 458.7 and 464.4 eV for TiO2 in Fig. 4B are belonged to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, respectively [51]. For MIP-TiO2-x and MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs, the peaks of Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 shift to lower binding energies and compose of four peaks. There are four peaks at 455.8, 458.3, 461.7 and 463.9 eV in MIP-TiO2-x, corresponding to Ti3+ 2p3/2, Ti4+ 2p3/2, Ti3+ 2p1/2 and Ti4+ 2p1/2, respectively, which confirms the existence of Ti3+ ions. Compared with MIP-TiO2-x, the peak area ratio of Ti3+ 2p3/2 (at ca. 455.6 eV)/Ti4+ 2p3/2 and Ti3+ 2p1/2 (at ca. 460 eV)/Ti4+ 2p1/2 are larger in MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs, indicating the Ti3+ signals are strengthened positively with the modification of CQDs. Because of its special electron structure, CQDs’ work function might be lower than that of MIP-TiO2-x, causing the transfer of delocalized electrons from CQDs to MIP-TiO2-x, which results in the MIP-TiO2-x/ CQDs be negatively charged and produces more Ti3+ defect [52]. It is reported that the presence of Ti3+ defect can act as active sites for oxygen adsorption and for trapping electrons from the recombination of electrons and holes, thus improving catalytic activity [52–56].

In terms of O1s revealed in Fig. 4C, the observed peaks at 529.6 eV and 530.5 eV in TiO2 are corresponding to the crystal lattice oxygen species (Ti-Olattice) and hydroxyl oxygen species (Ti-OHsurface), respectively [41,53]. After imprinted with MB molecules, the peaks slightly shifts to lower binding energy, and a third peak at about 531.5 eV is fitted, which is ascribed to the surface defects of oxygen vacancy (Ovacancy) near Ti3+ ions [51]. This is a convincing evidence of the Ti3+ presence. The peak area ratios of Ti-OHsurface/Ti-Olattice and Ovacancy/Ti-Olattice of MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs are higher than that of MIP-TiO2-x, which means MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs has more hydroxyl oxygen species and oxygen vacancies [54], and is in good accordance with the above analysis result about CQDs. The photogenerated holes can be trapped by the hydroxyl oxygen species and form oxidative hydroxyl radicals, and oxygen vacancies can be electron trapping sites. Both of them are conducive to the separation of photogenerated carriers [41,51]. Therefore, the MIP-TiO2-x and MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs are expected to provide better photocatalytic performance.

In Fig. 4D, the presence of Ti3+ and oxygen vacancy is further supported by ESR. As we can see, there is no signal found for the TiO2, while two paramagnetic signals are detected in MIP-TiO2-x and MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs. One symmetrical peaks at g = 2.002 correspond to oxygen vacancy and the other symmetrical peaks with g value of 1.976 confirm the existence of Ti3+ [41,57]. Furthermore, the ESR intensities of Ti3+ and oxygen vacancy are enhanced after the addition of carbon dots which suggested carbon dots could promote the formation of Ti3+ ions and oxygen vacancy.

The optical absorption behaviors of TiO2, MIP-TiO2-x and MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs were investigated by UV–vis diffuse reflection spectra. In Fig. 5 A, the as-prepared TiO2 can only absorb the light below 387.5 nm. After imprinted with MB molecules cavities, the absorption edge of MIP-TiO2-x and MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs are extended to the visible light even to the near-infrared area because of the generation of Ti3+ ions and oxygen vacancies [39–41]; and with the addition of CQDs, the absorption area and intensity are enhanced further ascribe to the promoted formation of Ti3+ ions and the TiCO bond between the TiO2 and carbon dots [53,58,59]. The bandgap values can be calculated by the UV–vis diffuse reflection spectra, and the corresponding bandgaps are displayed in Fig. 5B. Compared with the TiO2, the bandgaps of molecule imprinted TiO2 become narrower, which are 1.85 eV for MIP-TiO2-x and 1.54 eV for MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs. Narrowed bandgap means MIP-TiO2-x and MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs are more active in the visible light region and could be beneficial for improving their photocatalytic performance. In Fig. 5C, we can see the valance bands of TiO2, MIP-TiO2-x and MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs are 2.71, 2.65 and 2.50 eV, respectively. This is probably caused by the disordered layer outside the MIP-TiO2-x nanoparticles and the generated defects that could form some localized states between the valence and conduction band edge, result in the narrowed bandgap and increased response to visible light [55,56,59]. Based on the aforementioned XPS and ESR results of MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs, the further decreased band gap could result from the special chemical connection TiC bond, which produced more Ti3+ ions and generated defective energy level under conduction band [52,60]. The detailed energy band structures are illustrated in Fig. 5D. These localized states could benefit to the separation of photogenerated carriers and thus improve the transfer efficiency of photoelectrons, enhance the life-times of photogenerated carriers, eventually be advantageous to the improvement of photocatalytic performance.

To testify the photoelectric response ability of MIP-TiO2-x and MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs under visible light, photoelectron trapping experiment was conducted by using TEMPO as electron scavenger [61,62]. Generally, TEMPO has a stable triplet ESR signal, and it reacts with photogenerated electrons and gives a hydroxylamine (TEMPOH), which weakens the ESR signal. As we can see in Fig. 6 , TEMPO has a stable triplet ESR signal without photocatalyst. The ESR signal does not change obviously for TiO2, while subsequent attenuation of the TEMPO signal are observed for MIP-TiO2-x and MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs indicated that both of them can be excited under visible light and generate electrons. For MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs, about 52.64% of the signal intensity was deceased during visible light irradiation, while 37.8% signal intensity was reduced for MIP-TiO2-x, indicating the photogeneration of electrons for MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs was enhanced greatly after the modification with carbon dots. These results indicate that MIP-TiO2-x and MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs would be more photocatalytically active than TiO2 under visible light, and they are also in line with the diffuse reflectance UV–vis spectra result.

Black TiO2 can be prepared through introducing surface defects, such as hydrogenation or reduction by H2, metal or other reducing agent [18–20,63]. In this work, the presence of Ti3+ and oxygen vacancy in MIP-TiO2-x and MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs have been detected. It was supposed that MB had a dual role in the formation of MIP-TiO2-x. On the one hand, MB served as molecule imprinter in the process of sol–gel synthesis, producing imprinted cavities to the TiO2 surface. On the other, MB together with its decomposition products, in the 500 °C calcination stage, may act as a reducing agent and create oxygen vacancies and Ti3+ in TiO2 [41,63]. In addition, the color of MIP-TiO2-x and MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs maintained unchanged after 12 months at ambient condition, suggesting its satisfactory stability.

We optimized the adding amount of imprinted molecules and CQDs by comparing the degradation efficiency of catalysts to methylene blue. In Fig. 7 A, with the increased amount of imprinted molecules, the imprinted cavities on the surface increases, the specific surface area is improved and the number of Ti3+, oxygen vacancy and surface hydroxyl groups rises, which make the adsorption of MB increased and the recombination of photogenerated electrons-holes hampered, consequently the photocatalytic effect is improved; However, too much imprinted molecules may lead to the overlap of imprinted cavities on the TiO2 surface, resulting in the decrease of specific surface area. Too many Ti3+ and oxygen vacancies would act as recombination centers that will make MIP-TiO2-x less efficient in photocatalytic process. Fig. 7B shows the influence of the CQDs adding amount on the performance of MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs. It can be seen from the figure that MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs has the best catalytic degradation effect on MB when the addition amount of CQDs is 0.03 g. As the CQDs addition further increases, CQDs would aggregate on the surface without contacting with TiO2 that will introduce new defects (or act as recombination center), leading to the decline of photocatalytic performance [38]. Based on the above results, the MIP-TiO2-x and MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs prepared by adding 0.5 g MB or/and 0.03 g CQDs will be discussed in the following analysis.

The photocatalytic MB degradation performances of TiO2, MIP-TiO2-x and MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs were evaluated under the visible light (Fig. 8 A). MB may possibly self-degrade under visible-light, thus a blank test without photocatalyst was also carried out for comparison. It is found that only ca. 9% of MB is removed under visible light irradiation for 120 min. As expected, the molecularly imprinted composites exhibit much higher photocatalytic performance than that of TiO2 (15.3%). The degradation efficiency of MIP-TiO2-x and MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs are 78.7% and 92.6%, respectively. The degradation rate constant k of these three samples was displayed in Fig. 8B. The k of MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs is 0.018 min−1, which is 1.6 and 9 times to those of MIP-TiO2-x (k = 0.011 min−1) and TiO2 (k = 0.002 min−1), respectively. The results are consistent with predictions from previous tests.

We investigated the stability and regeneration potential of MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs. The used MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs was recovered by cleaning with ethanol and centrifugation for 3–4 times, drying in a vacuum oven at 80 °C, then can be used again. As observed in Fig. 8C, the MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs can be reused for 5 times with only slightly reduction in the degradation performance, and there is no obvious change happened in the compared XRD patterns (see Fig. 8D) after five photocatalysis cycles, which indicates its good chemical stability. The FTIR patterns of the MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs were also investigated after five recycling runs. As shown in Fig. S2, the FTIR patterns of MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs before and after reaction reveal that the phase and structure remained unchanged. Therefore, it is evident that the MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs does not undergo any photocorrosion or photobleaching during the degradation experiments. For comparison, the degradation efficiency of methylene blue with various photocatalyst under visible light are listed in Table 1 .

Change of solution pH would alter the surface charge density of photocatalyst, affect the adsorption ability to target pollutant and further influence the photodegradation activity [74]. In Fig. 9 A, we investigated the zeta potential at different pH values to determine the isoelectric point of MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs. As we can see, the surface isoelectric point of MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs is at pH = ca.6.6. According to the isoelectric point, when pH < 6.6, the surface of MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs is positively charged, which is conducive to the adsorption of anionic pollutant; When pH > 6.6, the catalyst surface is negatively charged, which favors to absorb cationic pollutant. When the pH value approaches 6.6, the surface charge of MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs will theoretically approach zero. Therefore, it is easier to understand the effect of varied pH values on k of the best photocatalyst MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs (in Fig. 9B). When pH = 9, MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs has the best photocatalytic effect on MB, and k reaches the maximum value. There are two reasons: Firstly, with the increase of pH, the surface of MIP-TiO2-x/ CQDs gradually becomes negatively charged, which enhances the electrostatic interaction between MB cation and photocatalyst surface, and improves the MB adsorption capacity; Secondly, alkaline condition is conducive to the generation of hydroxyl radicals, which are largely responsible for the degradation organic pollutant [75,76]. However, the pH goes higher, the catalyst surface would adsorb more dye, which subsequently makes the surface less exposed to the visible. Therefore, the creation of
OH is reduced, i.e., it makes the oxidation reaction slower and decreases the MB degradation efficiency. On the contrary, when the pH is lower than the isoelectric point, the surface of MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs gradually becomes positively charged, which induces the electrostatic repulsion to the MB cation, the MB adsorption amount decreases, thus the photocatalytic effect becomes poor, the k value decreases.

In order to exclude the effect of pH on the selective adsorption and degradation of MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs, the pH is not adjusted, and the initial value is around 6.8. At this point, the surface charge of MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs is theoretically approach zero. There is no electrostatic effect on methyl orange anion dye and methylene blue cationic dye. Fig. 10 A shows the absorption selectivity of MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs towards MB and methylene orange (MO). It can be seen that MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs shows a much higher adsorption capacity for MB than that for MO, indicating that MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs owned molecular recognized ability after imprinting MB molecules, which is due to the imprinted cavities on the TiO2 surface and their chemical interaction with the imprinted molecules. Adsorption capacity has important effect on the photodegradation performance of target pollutants. Therefore, in Fig. 10B, as we expected the degradation of MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs for MB is 93.1%, while there is only 59.4% for MO. The improved selectivity could be due to the matching cavities left by the imprinted molecules and the chemical interactions between the target pollutant and imprinted cavities.

To identifying the involved reactive species during degradation process, we carried out the ESR measurements under visible light irradiation. In Fig. 11 A, there are sextet strong characteristic signals of the DMPO−
O2 − after adding MIP-TiO2-x and MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs, which indicate the generation of superoxides. The typical quartet signals of DMPO−
OH are detected too (see Fig. 11B), suggesting that there are hydroxyl radicals involved in the system. When CPH is exposed to the presence of MIP-TiO2-x and MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs, we also observe ESR spectra consisting of triplet peaks (see Fig. 11C), demonstrating the presence of photogenerated holes. These results prove that pure TiO2 does not respond to the visible light at first but after being imprinted with MB molecules, the generated defects formed localized states between the valence and conduction band edge and resulted in the narrowed bandgap and the increased response to visible light. More importantly, CQDs decorated MIP-TiO2-x generate superoxides, hydroxyl radicals and holes more effectively during photoexcitation, which also back up the fact that the MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs gives the best catalytic performance. Radical scavenge experiments for MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs were performed to evaluate the importance of these reactive species. As displayed in Fig. 11D, the inhibition of BQ on photocatalytic degradation efficiency is the most pronounced, followed by IPA and MEOH, indicating
O2 − and −
OH play synergetic role and are largely responsible for the degradation of MB.

To better understand the mechanism of efficient degradation of MB, the recombination of charge carries was studied by photoluminescence analysis. Generally, lower PL intensity means less serious the recombination of charge carriers is. In Fig. 12 A, the PL intensity of MIP-TiO2-x and MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs are lower than that of TiO2 because of the abundant Ti3+ ions, oxygen vacancies and the surface hydroxyl groups [41,51,56]. Firstly, the electrons that are excited to the conduction band and captured by the defective states below the conduction band, rather than transitioning back to the valence band and recombining with the holes. Secondly, the surface hydroxyl groups can trap holes to form hydroxyl radicals suppressing the electron-hole recombination. Thus, the PL intensity decreased, and further reduced after modification with CQDs that is due to the increased amount of Ti3+ ions and oxygen vacancies and CQDs which can also effectively promote the separation of electrons-holes[38,52,77]. The recombination of charge carriers becomes less serious, suggesting that more photogenerated electrons can participate in the photodegradation process. The transient photocurrent response results under visible light are shown in Fig. 12B. The highest photocurrent density is observed in MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs, implying that the maximum number of electrons produced in MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs can be efficiently separated and then effectively used to remove MB. Further, as demonstrated in Fig. 12C, the arc radius of MIP-TiO2-x decreases compared to TiO2, and it reaches to a minimum in MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs, which indicates the separation of photogenerated carriers is more effective and the efficiency of inside charge immigration is higher in MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs that correspond to the transient photocurrent response. More effective separation of photogenerated carriers and faster charge transfer that could prolong the photogenerated carriers’ lifetime, all of these account for the improved photocatalytic degradation activity of MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs.

Based on the aforementioned experiment results, MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs performs the best photodegradation ability for MB, and the possible mechanism is illustrated in Fig. 13 . Firstly, after calcination, the template was removed and the imprinted cavities were left on the TiO2 surface, which increased the specific surface area and gave the adsorption selectivity. Secondly, except serving as molecule imprinter, MB together with its decomposition products during calcination stage acted as reducing agent and created oxygen vacancies, Ti3+ and surface hydroxyl groups in TiO2. Ti3+ and oxygen vacancies introduced some localized states between the valence and conduction band edge as captures of photoelectrons, effectively restraining the recombination of photoelectrons and holes, and resulted in the narrowed bandgap and the increased photoelectric response to visible light range. The hydroxyl group on the surface can capture the holes and react to form hydroxyl radicals, which are conducive to inhibiting recombination. Thirdly, after decoration with CQDs, the CQDs were connected with MIP-TiO2-x via TiCO bond, some delocalized electron of CQDs migrated to TiO2, producing more Ti3+ and oxygen vacancy defects in MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs. However, too many Ti3+ and oxygen vacancies would act as recombination centers, which will make MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs less efficient in photocatalytic process. In the oxidation reaction, the holes react with TiOH bonds or hydroxide ions to produce
OH, while the electrons from the Ti3+ and oxygen vacancies reduce the absorbed O2 to reactive
O2 −. Both
O2 − and
OH are strong oxidation species, which can efficiently degrade the target pollutant. In short, the remarkable performance of MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs for MB degradation under visible light is attributed to the broadened light-response range due to the Ti3+ ions and the oxygen vacancies, the increased specific surface area and the selective adsorption achieved by molecular imprinting, efficient photogenerated carriers’ movement thanks to the CQDs modification and synergism among Ti3+ ions, oxygen vacancies and TiOH bonds.

In summary, MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs nanocomposites are firstly synthesized using methylene blue as molecular imprinter and reducer via hydrothermal-calcination method. Methylene blue has a double effect on the formation of MIP-TiO2-x: (1) Alter the morphology and structure, increasing the specific surface area and endowing the ability of selective adsorption; (2) Generate Ti3+ and oxygen vacancies, enhancing the utilization of visible light and suppressing the photoelectrons-holes recombination process. The introduction of CQDs is icing on the cake, this special chemical connection between CQDs and TiO2 via TiCO bond further extends the optical response range, hinders the recombination of photogenerated carriers and improves the photocatalytic performance. This work not only provides a molecularly imprinted black MIP-TiO2-x/CQDs nanocomposite material to efficiently photodegrade target pollutant, but also confirms that perfect fusion of molecular imprinting and carbon dots could be a promising solution for the waste-water purification.
